speaker: interviewer
text: Alex Chevion interview, tape four. Mr. Chevion, you just described the surgery you had to conceal your identity. Can you continue?

speaker: interviewee
text: Right. Now my name was Jan Migon. Those were the papers from which I had my name.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you spell it?

speaker: interviewee
text: Jan, J-A-N, Migon-- Migon, M-I-G-O-N. As time went on, I got used more and more to that name, and I tried as much as possible to convince myself that that's my real name, you know, so that in a normal day-to-day situation when you encounter people, I would be able to face questions or interrogations without panicking, and just naturally say that this is my name. Part of that, also to try to erase from memory my whole childhood, my whole being, my-- my whole existence prior to that, you know.

speaker: interviewee
text: Because indeed, every once in a while, I had to undergo strange tests. And I was able to pass the tests. For instance, the German suddenly announced that every young boy, age of 18, 19, or 20-- I forgot exactly, and I was in that category at that point with my papers-- has to register with the Labor Department. You see? And I went to register. And as I registered, I listened to other people talking and all that. And it was clear that those people will be sent to Germany to work, build some fortifications or whatever. They weren't treated like Jews, like slave laborers. But they were more treated like recruits in the army, except they wouldn't be in the-- in-- in the army-- but in the labor battalion. And I befriended one of the clerks there who was in charge of that registration. I talked with him and all that. And finally I asked him, can you help me, you know? And out of that conversation, he gave me a tip. Try to do this and that, and gave me back my form which I filled out. And told me, to try to fill out something else. Then this might help you, because those we might not take or whatever. So here I was with a new name, making new friends, facing a new challenge under this new name as a Pole, you know? And nobody uncovered at the time a Jew. And it's things like that which gave me confidence.

speaker: interviewee
text: However, at one point I got into a certain business venture. This person who, where I lived, had friends in the refinery. And they were working in an asphalt pit, where he was buying asphalt for them for this factory where he was working. And I, at that point, had a job which I got through the newspapers. You know, I got a regular job. And in the course of that job, I met a man from Warsaw who was very much interested in buying this kind of asphalt. And I organized a system whereby through his contacts, I would be getting that asphalt from the refinery, sort of like a black market operation. Because, by the way, the Germans would not allow the Poles to buy anything officially or legitimately. The only way they could live is only through the black market.

speaker: interviewee
text: So everything was on the black market. The whole economy was geared that everybody would steal part of what he was involved, to the extent that he could, and sell it on the black market. In this case, I organized the stealing of that asphalt, you know, and shipping it to Warsaw, and selling it through that man. And this developed into a quite profitable business. And everything was fine. But mostly what I liked about it, it gave me a cover for my existence.

speaker: interviewee
text: Now, I would take that trip in that truck, and sit in the cabin with two drivers, you see, in-- in between them. One would be driving. I would be sitting in the middle. The next one would sit at the door. And either I fell asleep and mumbled something, or they by close observation over such a long trip, somehow they suspected that I'm Jewish. And they tipped off their friends, who came one day, and acted as policemen, and entered the house, and flashed a-- you know, document. We are from the police. And started to interrogate me, and then they say, come with us. And I'm going with them.

speaker: interviewee
text: And here's my suitcase which I took. And the suitcase had lots of money. And they saw that the suitcase had lots of money. And as we go and go, and I kind of wonder to myself, shall I run, or shall I not run? Shall I run? If I run, they'll catch me and all that. And as we keep coming closer and closer, suddenly they kind of suggest to me that if I run for my life or something like that, they might not do anything, just because I'm a nice person. In short, I recognize that they were not real policemen. They were just blackmailing me, because, you know. And this later I found out from many friends was-- was the common procedure that many people suddenly engaged in catching Jews who are hiding as I did, and blackmailing them, you know? And what choice did I have? You know, now, I didn't want to give them my suitcase with the money. And they tried to make me run for my life and leave the suitcase with them. At one point, when they finally got totally disappointed with me, they simply grabbed the suitcase and ran. Instead of me running for my life, they were running for their life, you know? So this is one incident where I got caught.

speaker: interviewee
text: And after the war, I read a book by Ringelblum, who was a historian before the war. Who was the only-- he's the only one who wrote a book-- he didn't survive. But he wrote a book, and his book was hidden in a milk can. And his book survived. That's the only book which was written by a-- by a person during the war. Because we all wrote books. Many wrote books after the war. But that's the only book. Which-- now in that book, he describes is that woman for whom it was much easier to pretend that they are 'Aryan,' quote, unquote meaning non-Jewish.

speaker: interviewee
text: Many of them would go and try to do what I did. And then they would come back to the ghetto, and said, no more. And the reason they did that, because the constant tension and fear of being caught, A. B, as soon as they would leave the ghetto, there would be Poles lined up there waiting to go right after them and try to blackmail them. Well, we know you are Jewish and all that. Do you know what I mean? And they felt so miserable about this that they would rather go back to the ghetto and not try. So what I did was in that sense very difficult, and there were thousands which must have done that. Not when I did it, but later on as everything became hopeless, as the only way to survive actually was either to go to Auschwitz with very few chances for survival or else try to pretend to be a non-Jew, or find a Pole which will hide you out.

speaker: interviewee
text: Now out of those three categories, those which found Poles that hide them out, sooner or later most of them didn't survive. Because the Poles will take their money. And when the money finished, they would stop hiding them. In other cases, they would do it simply out of fear. In another case, they would tell a best friend or a neighbor or a relative, yes, I'm hiding out Jews. And before they knew, everybody would tell a friend. And before you know, the police would found out, so forth. So very few survived. Those which did what I did, had to have what they called looks, which means good looks, to have the kind of looks which suggest everything but Jewish. The Poles had an image in their mind of-- of Jewish looks, you know. And if somebody didn't fit that image, they find it very difficult to believe that he's Jewish.

speaker: interviewee
text: And among those that did it like me, who were even very successful, also only a small percentage survive. And that was only luck, sheer luck. And in my case, probably the fact that I left Poland in 1943 in July. Had I stayed for another two years, who knows? I might have been caught. Every day my chances were even-- whatever they were-- that I will be caught. So it-- it was extraordinary luck. Plus the-- the cunning, the schtick, the-- the game that you play, you know, and so forth. And you had to be good at it. And as time went on, I apparently became good at it. Tell me about your father and your brother.

speaker: interviewee
text: Now, my father was hiding out in a place, for which he was paying big money, just so that this-- this woman was hiding him out. But one day, she had to move from that place. Because the Germans-- they were enlarging the base. It was a military base, an airplane base, and so whatever. And they issued an edict, everybody from this area has to move. My father had nowhere to go. He couldn't find another place to hide out.

speaker: interviewer
text: What-- this was in Lwow?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was in Lwow. And I was in Warsaw. And I knew at that point about that-- about the possibility of going to Hungary. Because a friend of mine-- who by the way also underwent the same operation from the same surgeon-- went to Hungary. He went in May, and wrote me a card. We agreed on a certain code, which would indicate that it's safe to do this. So he wrote me back a card from Budapest that he arrived to Budapest. So I knew about this possibility.

speaker: interviewee
text: And here my father, being totally exposed-- in other words, he would have perished right there and then. Because he could not do what I did. A, he didn't have yet the papers. And to arrange for forged papers at that point became more and more difficult, you know?

speaker: interviewee
text: Plus, he just didn't have the looks. Plus, he didn't have the-- the-- the-- the means to play that game. You know, he was maybe too old, and it just wouldn't have worked. So, he was the reason that I went to Hungary. Because the only way to save him was for me to undertake that trip to Hungary.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you get from Lwow to Warsaw? And what happened also--

speaker: interviewee
text: I have a map there, which I could show you, if you wish me to go up. I've prepared that map. I went. I had a smuggler, whom I found out through that man. And incidentally, he found out about the smuggler. In other words, before this man-- his name is Marguilies-- before him, somebody by the name of Landau who had a son in Israel, got a letter from his son in Israel to contact a certain smuggler who will help him to get to Hungary.

speaker: interviewee
text: So Mr. Landau went to Hungary, and sent a card to Mr. Marguilies, I arrived. And Mr. Marguilies went to Hungary, and sent a card to me and told me, I arrived. But before he left, he told me the name of that smuggler and how to contact him. And I contacted that smuggler. Right? And he told me, here is the deal. It costs you that and that much money. It was a lot of money. Do you remember how much?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, but it was a lot. In other words, most people probably couldn't afford it. I was fortunate I had the money. Plus, he said, look. All I'm doing for you is you never shake hands with me. You never know me. If I'm ever asked, I never saw you. I never heard of you. Don't dare to come even close to me. But keep watching. I'll be in this car. You enter this car. When I am supposed to jump, I'll pass to your car and make something-- I forgot the signal, the arranged signal was-- you get ready. And then you look out through the window. When you see me jumping, jump right after me. And then as soon after you jump, I run away and hide there in the, you know, grass. And you go there and hide in the grass. And then I go through the border, but you are always like 50 feet behind me. Never dare to come even close. And at one point, I'll bring you to a spot, and I'll show you lights dimming in the far side. That's Slovakia already. You go to that village where those lights there. Now, the story about Slovakia was that the policeman in that particular village again decided to wash off his sins. He killed a lot of Jews in the beginning of the war, to wash off his sins. And everybody knew at that point that Hitler lost the war. This is 1943, July, and about two months after the ghetto uprising, but six months after Stalingrad. It was clear that Hitler is losing the war.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long had you been in Warsaw? We didn't get that clear.

speaker: interviewee
text: I was in Warsaw from-- I came to Lwow in June of '22-- and I left--

speaker: interviewee
text: '42, And I left for Warsaw in about August-- October-- September. September of '42. So I was in Warsaw from September of '42 till July of '43, seven-- 10 months.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did-- why did you have to leave Lwow? Was it because you were--

speaker: interviewee
text: I didn't have to leave Lwow. But I was looking for a-- I-- I got a job in Lwow. And I worked in that job. And I worked for the three or four months that I worked there, and I met that person who was interested in buying asphalt. And because I knew that the person in whose house I lived at that time, who my father's friend, was buying asphalt, and he knew about it, how to arrange it. And in talking to him, I found out that I can organize that deal. And that made me go to Warsaw. At first I just traveled with the truck, and I would travel back to Lwow. And then one day I asked him if I could rent an apartment. And he recommended me to a lady who had a big apartment, but needed a tenant just-- to make a livelihood. And that way, she didn't even ask me if I'm Jewish. Because if he recommend me, how could I be Jewish, and so forth. And I managed to rent that apartment from her. And I felt that will be safe, even safer than the apartment I had in Lwow. Also his wife was very afraid of me being in her house. She agreed to keep me. But she kept begging me, day after day after day that I should look out for another apartment.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember their names?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. It was many years. And she insisted that I-- and she had problems. She was hemorrhaging and bleeding, and she blamed those problems on the fear that I'm living in the house. So I was under pressure to find myself another apartment. So that's reason number two. Although he didn't tell me, leave tomorrow. But I was under notice. And here, I managed to rent that apartment in Warsaw in such a way, where, you know. So-- so for these reasons, I moved over to Warsaw.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then what happened there? Continue.

speaker: interviewee
text: Now, in Warsaw, I lived in Warsaw. And basically I had-- the-- the game I was playing was I had two names and two apartments. One, what I called my real name, which was Jan Migon. The other name was Jan [? Povloski, ?] which had a forged identity card. See, forged in the sense that my identity card was obtained under false pretenses, but it was not forged. It was issued in a genuine way by the police. The other one was totally forged. I just bought it from forgers. Now, the two apartments helped for two reasons. A, if for some reason I would be compromised or caught and managed still to escape with my life, I couldn't go back to this apartment, because as they caught me, they identified my address. So I would have at least where to sleep the next night. But two, even more importantly was where I lived I pretended that I am actually a resident in Lwow. And I'm only in Warsaw on business. And the other apartment, I pretended the same story. But as I moved from this apartment, I moved into the other apartment, and back and forth in that way. You know, I kept-- and I kept myself busy. Every day I would leave the house in the morning, and come back at night. And I had to spend the whole day someplace in the city, you know. Lots of hours I would spend in the tramway, which is a bus, a public bus. But, you know, electrical-driven car, a trolley car. And the trolleys were packed full. So it was safe. You know, you couldn't talk to anybody. Because you were that packed up, you know. Or you could ask maybe a question. But you couldn't carry on a conversation. I would go to movies very often, and spend hours in movies, but always enter when it's dark, and try to leave before the lights came on. And then go back to another movie, and do the same thing. I would find parks, which were kind of secluded and deserted, and spend hours in parks. But I had to do something with myself for a whole day. That was tough. That wasn't easy. But I did it day after day after day.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you do any business or any work? Did you have any-- how did have money?

speaker: interviewee
text: I did-- well, I had money. Because I got money from when I left the house, my mother gave me some money. But I made a lot of money on that asphalt business, which I kept doing, even after I was uncovered once. I got just a new truck, and I kept doing it with new truckers, you know? And this was a very profitable business it so happens. Because anything which had to do with the black market was extremely profitable. Part of that business was also, as-- as we were traveling in that truck, this distance from Lwow to Warsaw is probably 300 miles. And it took like a whole night, or a whole day's travel. So on the way, we had to stop in a few places. So we would stop in villages, and buy flour. The farmers would sell flour, all on the black market, you know. And we would buy that flour, and then sell it in the city, you know. So that was profitable. So it was a profitable business. And I made money. I had a lot of money. I know that I had a couch like that. And the whole couch, the floor was 500-- the highest bill, was the nomination was 500 zloty. And I had packets of these 500 zloty laying in that couch. As a matter of fact-- and that's a side story. I was looking to exchange these zlotys for dollars. And I was willing to buy it from a black marketeer. But there was a lot of dollars were falsified. Until one day, through a friend of a friend, I was told that somebody in the bank, in an official German bank, was selling dollars for zlotys, on the black market again. And I really purchased dollars from him. And those dollars, I managed to smuggle all the way through Slovakia and Hungary. And I finally came to Israel. And the dollars were in the heel. Some of them were still in the heel of my boots. And I took them out. And I went to Barclays Bank, which is a big British bank. And there was a problem that the money was so-- so squeezed tight, by the time we opened it, it had a lot of holes. But it was genuine. And they paid me based on the remaining area of the money like, 78% of the area was still preserved. They paid me in full for that money.

speaker: interviewee
text: So that's just to show that under the German eyes, there is somebody in the bank was running his own business selling dollars. And this was not unusual. It sounds unusual today. But the whole economy, everything was black market, total black market. So how long were you in Warsaw?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was in Warsaw nine months from-- from September 1942 till July of 1943, which is about for 11 months.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you spent your time--

speaker: interviewee
text: And I spent my time--

speaker: interviewer
text: --roaming.

speaker: interviewee
text: I had this business, which in other words, every two weeks I would go for this type of a shipment, or every three weeks. Sometimes maybe every month. So I don't remember. But there was a number of these shipments, each one very profitable. Other than that, I spent my time roaming through the city. Not in the house, because that would have been too suspicious.

speaker: interviewee
text: Both the neighbors and the landlady would have suspected, here, what's this man hiding here? So that was the biggest problem, what to do with myself during the whole day, you see? What did you know or hear about the Warsaw ghetto, living--

speaker: interviewee
text: Now, I was-- as I was traveling in the trolley, which was packed, as the trolley was passing by certain streets, you could hear shots from the ghetto, sporadic like gunshots, not put-put-put like from a machine gun, but a gunshot here, and then later another gunshot, a gunshot, a gunshot. And the people we're talking about it. Now true, the comments were terrible. This unnerved me so much. You know, in Poland, the-- the Poles used their standard jokes about Jews, mostly about the fact that they're cowards and that they never held a rifle in their hands, you know, and this type of stuff. Those were pre-1939 pre-war jokes about Jews. And they kept repeating the same jokes, how a Jew is handling a rifle, how a Jew is shooting.

speaker: interviewee
text: Here is such a heroic effort, people, the only people who were standing the German army, the Poles didn't dare do that. You know, I mean, in Europe practically at that point, including France and everybody else, nobody stood up to the Germans. Everybody surrendered. And here's a group of people who dare to do that. You know? So I remember that vividly.

speaker: interviewee
text: Another thing which I remember is at one point as I was walking the streets, I arrived at a corner where there I saw of group of Polish women. And they were very excited. And I come close, and I-- it's a crowd. You know, like maybe at that point, it was like 30 or 40 people. I entered the crowd and I listened. They were talking about that the house in the distance which they saw, which was across a wall, which was a house inside the ghetto. They saw Jewish women throwing themselves with children from the upper floor. That was-- I did not see that picture. But they were recounting that picture, you know. So that's the only other thing. And then one more incident, after the uprising which finished in May, as I walked once on the street, I suddenly saw two people emerge like from a sewer, you know, from a-- you could see a deck lifted. And they were running for their life someplace, you know. I saw that. Which fits the stories, which you subsequently read about the ghetto, you know. So.

speaker: interviewee
text: But I did not have any contact, or I didn't fight in the ghettos, or no way, you know. Of course, I dreamt, I wished I could have done. But what could I have done? There's nothing I could have done. I didn't have armaments. I didn't have a gun. I didn't have connections anywhere to buy a gun. And I didn't know how to even enter the ghetto, if I wanted to do that, which would have been suicidal.

speaker: interviewee
text: But, you know, even if I wanted to do that, there was no way I could do that.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you see it from where you were?

speaker: interviewee
text: I did not see-- you see, the ghetto was cordoned off. And-- and I didn't even know how to go to the ghetto. I would not-- haven't been born in Warsaw, and only living eight months, I didn't know enough of Warsaw. And there was nobody I would dare to ask, how do you get to the ghetto. So I really wouldn't have known even how to get there.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you were in Warsaw until July?

speaker: interviewee
text: Until July.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then?

speaker: interviewee
text: One more incident, which I want to recount about Warsaw, which is sort of interesting, also about which I read recently. At one point I found out, for I had contact in Warsaw, very careful prearranged contact with my brother and two other people. One was named Engelhart, and one named Marguilies. And we would meet occasionally, but very careful. Because we always were afraid that people observing us from a distance together might suspect that-- you know. Now one day I found out-- and I don't remember if it was from my brother or from Mr. Engelhart or Mr. Marguilies, that you can go to a certain hotel, and you'll obtain there South American papers, a passport, a South American passport. And the Germans respect that passport, respect as a foreign citizen you will be safe. Actually, it was a ruse, which the Germans arranged. And they spread that rumor. And none of these people survived. And just recently, I read an account of what happened to them. It's a long story. But, you know, so these sorts of things were going on.

speaker: interviewer
text: So continue with what happened to you.

speaker: interviewee
text: Now, then when I left, and as I said, we went on a train, me and my father. And again, by the way, me and my father didn't talk. When we sat on the train, we pretended like we don't know each other. Because any time you associated with somebody closely, the people watching you two can deduct all kinds of things. This was considered very unsafe. So we are on that train.

speaker: interviewer
text: From--

speaker: interviewee
text: My father--

speaker: interviewer
text: --how did you get your father to Warsaw?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father came from Lwow, again, with my sister's help. But again, she kind of prearranged with him, how to guide him, but not to be seen close to him. She brought him to Krakow on a certain hour, on a certain train. And I came from Warsaw, on a certain hour, on a certain train, and together with my father entered that train, but separately. And we go on that train--

speaker: interviewer
text: How were you able to communicate with your--

speaker: interviewee
text: Letters. The post office worked. The post office worked. You know, people constantly wrote letters. And there was still businesses. An economy was going on. So they had to maintain the mail. That's how we communicated. No telephone. None of us had a telephone. You couldn't call up, just by letters.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we went on train, and according to the smuggler, the way he arranged it, right before Zakopane, we had to jump out from the train, a little station, a little village, and get into that grass. And that's what we did. And we followed him. And we followed through the mountains, until we came to the peak there, where on the other side was already Slovakia. Now, the story there in Slovakia was that there was the head of the police in that village, would-- if he intercepted any Jew, would bring him to Svaty Liptovsky Mikulas, or actually Liptovsky Svaty Mikulas. That's the full name. That's a little town in Slovakia. And there was one house there, and it had a large courtyard. And that house was a Jewish family, a rabbi, and maybe 50 Jews, right?

speaker: interviewee
text: And when we arrived, we were told, look, we have a deal was this policeman that you have to leave in two weeks. You can stay here two weeks, and not more. Because we constantly accept other people to arrive. And with him, we have an agreement. And we want to honor this agreement. That the total number of Jews in this house will never exceed XYZ. So you arrive now. You have to make space for others to arrive after you. So you must leave.

speaker: interviewee
text: And you leave towards Budapest, see? And they, again, told us that somebody, again a smuggler, will help us to go. We get on a train, and we go to Presov, which is quite a distance from that Svaty Liptovsky Mikulas. And in Presov, there will be somebody who will help us to smuggle the border into Hungary.

speaker: interviewee
text: Unfortunately, we managed to arrive to Slovakia safely, and we managed to cross practically all of Slovakia on the train to Presov. But that smuggler, instead of handing-- helping us, almost handed us over to the Hungarian police, and we got arrested and sent to jail in [NON-ENGLISH], which is a big jail, you know, in-- in Budapest, big, giant jail, heavy buildings.

speaker: interviewer
text: We'll pause here and continue in a moment.

speaker: interviewee
text: Right. Right.
